---
title: "How Evals & Observability Work, Visually"
subtitle: "One coffee shop, one bot: fixed test sets, judges, drift, spans, tokens and cost, offline scoring versus online tracing"
author: "Pranjal Rawat"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: black
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: true
    progress: true
    width: 1200
    height: 720
    margin: 0.055
    center: true
    navigation-mode: linear
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root {
  --bg: #16171a; --fg: #e6e6e9; --muted: #8b8b92; --sub: #a3a3ab;
  --accent: #7cb0de; --feat: #6bbf8a; --amber: #d9a441; --rule: #2a2c31;
  --node: #1d2733; --edge: #5a6472; --ink: #0f1114; --pink: #e06b9c;
  --violet: #b19cf5;
}
.reveal { font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif; }
.reveal, .reveal .slides section { background: var(--bg); color: var(--fg); }
.reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3 { color: var(--fg); font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: -0.5px; text-transform: none; }
.reveal h2 { font-size: 1.55em; border-bottom: 2px solid var(--rule); padding-bottom: 0.25em; margin-bottom: 0.5em; }
.reveal a { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal .slide-number { background: none; color: var(--muted); }
.reveal .footer, .reveal .slide-number { font-size: 12px; }
.reveal .subtle { color: var(--sub); font-size: 0.8em; }
.reveal .src { color: var(--muted); font-size: 0.55em; margin-top: 0.8em; }
.reveal .lead { font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.5; }
.reveal .def-box, .reveal .key-idea, .reveal .pitfall { background: var(--node); border-radius: 8px; padding: .5em .8em; margin: .4em 0; border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); font-size: .82em; line-height: 1.4; }
.reveal .key-idea { border-left-color: var(--feat); }
.reveal .pitfall  { border-left-color: var(--pink); }
.reveal .def-box::before  { content: "DEFINITION"; }
.reveal .key-idea::before { content: "KEY IDEA"; }
.reveal .pitfall::before  { content: "PITFALL"; }
.reveal .def-box::before,.reveal .key-idea::before,.reveal .pitfall::before { display:block; font-size:.62em; letter-spacing:1.5px; font-weight:700; color:var(--muted); margin-bottom:.25em; }
.reveal .taxonomy { display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(var(--cols,3),1fr); gap:.6em; margin-top:.5em; }
.reveal .taxonomy .cat { background:var(--node); border:1px solid var(--rule); border-top:3px solid var(--accent); border-radius:6px; padding:.5em .7em; font-size:.74em; }
.reveal .taxonomy .cat h4 { margin:0 0 .2em; color:var(--accent); font-size:1.05em; }
.reveal code { color: var(--feat); background: rgba(124,176,222,0.08); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 4px; }
.reveal section img { background: none; border: none; box-shadow: none; }
/* SVG shared visual language */
.fig { width: 100%; height: auto; max-height: 62vh; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.node { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; }
.node.feat { stroke: var(--feat); }
.node.hot { fill: #3a2f14; stroke: var(--amber); }
.node.ghost { fill: #191c20; stroke: var(--edge); }
.node.bad { stroke: var(--pink); }
.cap { fill: var(--fg); font: 16px -apple-system, sans-serif; text-anchor: middle; }
.cap.big { font-size: 21px; font-weight: 600; }
.zonelabel { fill: var(--muted); font: 600 14px -apple-system, sans-serif; letter-spacing: 1.5px; text-anchor: middle; }
.edge { stroke: var(--edge); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrow); }
.flow { stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrowA); stroke-dasharray: 6 5; }
.zone { fill: none; stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 12; }
.card { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 8; }
.card.hot { stroke: var(--amber); }
.card.ok { stroke: var(--feat); }
.card.bad { stroke: var(--pink); }
.cardtext { fill: var(--fg); font: 15px ui-monospace, monospace; }
.note { fill: var(--sub); font: 15px -apple-system, sans-serif; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.9); transition: all 0.3s ease; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in.visible { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); }
/* draw-on edges */
.edge.draw, .flow.draw { stroke-dasharray: 1; stroke-dashoffset: 1; }
.reveal .fragment.visible .edge.draw,
.reveal .fragment.visible .flow.draw,
.reveal .edge.draw.fragment.visible,
.reveal .flow.draw.fragment.visible { animation: gv-draw 0.55s ease both; }
@keyframes gv-draw { from { stroke-dashoffset: 1; } to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
.reveal .fragment.pop { opacity: 0; transform-box: fill-box; transform-origin: center; }
.reveal .fragment.pop.visible { animation: gv-pop 0.45s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.8,0.2,1) both; }
@keyframes gv-pop { 0% { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.5); } 60% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1.06); } 100% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); } }
</style>

<svg width="0" height="0" style="position:absolute" aria-hidden="true">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5a6472"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowA" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#7cb0de"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

# Two ways to ask "is it working" {.center}

::: lead
Grading a bot against a fixed test set answers one question: did it get
these known cases right. Watching a live run answers a different one:
what actually happened, span by span, token by token, dollar by dollar.
Most teams build only the first and are surprised when it doesn't explain
the second.
:::

**Offline, scored.** A fixed test set, a metric, a number that regresses or doesn't.
**Online, traced.** A live run, instrumented into spans, read after the fact.

::: notes
The deck is one long argument in two halves. Part 1 stays offline: a
frozen test set, deterministic metrics, judges, pairwise preference, and
the arithmetic of whether a drop is real. Part 2 makes the pivot: the
same bot, now watched as it runs, with token/cost/latency spans instead
of pass/fail rows. Part 3 runs the real libraries, deepeval, ragas,
langfuse, deterministically, no stub.
:::

---

## Offline-scored vs. online-traced

::: {.def-box}
**Offline-scored vs. online-traced.** Offline-scored means graded against
a test set with known-correct answers. Online-traced means watched as it
runs, with no answer key, via spans, tokens, and cost.
:::

---

## Meet Beanline Coffee, graded

Every figure ahead is a photograph of real code grading (or tracing) one small coffee shop's order-taking bot: a fixed set of orders, a scripted model that replies the same way every run, and real langchain/langgraph underneath.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="120" width="230" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="82" y="152">fixed test set</text>
    <text class="note" x="82" y="182" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">5 orders, known</text>
    <text class="note" x="82" y="200" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">expected answers</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="node" x="370" y="100" width="250" height="130" rx="12"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="495" y="150">Beanline's bot</text>
    <text class="note" x="495" y="185" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">ScriptedChatModel:</text>
    <text class="note" x="495" y="203" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">every prediction, replayed exactly</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card ok" x="700" y="90" width="250" height="150"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="722" y="122">two verdicts</text>
    <text class="note" x="722" y="150" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">offline: a score, a pass/fail row</text>
    <text class="note" x="722" y="172" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">online: a span tree, tokens, cost</text>
    <text class="note" x="722" y="194" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">both computed by real code</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 290 165 L 365 165"/>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 620 165 L 695 165"/>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="320">The model's words are scripted. Every metric, span, and dollar is computed for real.</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="355" text-anchor="middle">Exact match really compares strings, judges are scripted models through real judging code, spans really nest, tokens and cost really sum. Nothing is a mockup.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Same honesty contract as the sibling decks. The one fake is the model's
dialogue; every metric, judge verdict, statistic, span, and dollar figure
is computed by real code on that dialogue, which is exactly what makes
every figure assertable by an independent oracle.
:::

---

# Part 1 · Offline, scored {.center}

::: lead
A frozen test set with known-correct answers, and a metric that turns a
prediction into a number. Deterministic by construction: same script, same
score, every run.
:::

::: {.def-box}
**Test set.** A frozen list of (input, expected) pairs, checked into the
repo like code, the eval's ground truth. Byte-identical every run.
:::

---

## Grading needs an answer key

::: {.key-idea}
Grading needs an answer key. A reference-based metric is a pure function of
(prediction, expected). Without a known-correct answer to compare against,
it cannot be honest.
:::

---


# What Is a Test Set?

## What Is a Test Set? {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/what-is-a-test-set/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## What Is a Test Set? {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/what-is-a-test-set/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## What Is a Test Set? {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/what-is-a-test-set/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## What Is a Test Set? {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/what-is-a-test-set/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

## What Is a Test Set? {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/what-is-a-test-set/step-05.png){fig-align="center"}

## What Is a Test Set? {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/what-is-a-test-set/step-06.png){fig-align="center"}

## What Is a Test Set? {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/what-is-a-test-set/step-07.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## Two ways to check an answer

::: {.def-box}
**Exact match.** Does the prediction equal the expected string, verbatim.
The simplest metric, and the hardest one to argue with.
:::

::: {.def-box}
**Recall@k.** Of the items that were actually relevant, how many did the
top-k surface. Ragas's non-LLM version of this is `NonLLMContextRecall`.
:::

---


# Offline Metrics: Exact Match, Recall, and the BLEU Trap

## Offline Metrics: Exact Match, Recall, and the BLEU Trap {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/offline-metrics/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Offline Metrics: Exact Match, Recall, and the BLEU Trap {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/offline-metrics/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Offline Metrics: Exact Match, Recall, and the BLEU Trap {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/offline-metrics/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## Offline Metrics: Exact Match, Recall, and the BLEU Trap {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/offline-metrics/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

## Offline Metrics: Exact Match, Recall, and the BLEU Trap {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/offline-metrics/step-05.png){fig-align="center"}

## Offline Metrics: Exact Match, Recall, and the BLEU Trap {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/offline-metrics/step-06.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## The trap in surface metrics

::: {.pitfall}
**BLEU / ROUGE score a correct paraphrase low.** Two answers can share the
same meaning and almost no surface tokens, token overlap alone cannot
tell "reworded" from "wrong." Target what the answer actually claims (a
number, a fact), not its word choice.
:::

---


# LLM as Judge, and How It Fails

## LLM as Judge, and How It Fails {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/llm-as-judge/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## LLM as Judge, and How It Fails {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/llm-as-judge/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## LLM as Judge, and How It Fails {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/llm-as-judge/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## LLM as Judge, and How It Fails {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/llm-as-judge/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## A model grading a model

::: {.def-box}
**LLM-as-judge.** A second model reads a candidate answer against a rubric
and returns a verdict. Useful when there's no exact string to match
against, and only as reliable as what the rubric actually asks it to
check.
:::

::: {.pitfall}
**A judge asked to check agreement will rubber-stamp a false premise.**
Write rubrics against ground truth (the menu, the till, the stock room),
never against a claim the prompt itself supplied.
:::

---


# Pairwise Comparison: A vs B

## Pairwise Comparison: A vs B {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/pairwise-comparison/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Pairwise Comparison: A vs B {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/pairwise-comparison/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Pairwise Comparison: A vs B {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/pairwise-comparison/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## Three families of offline metric

::::: {.taxonomy style="--cols:3"}
::: {.cat}
`<h4>Reference-based</h4>`{=html}
Exact match, recall@k. Compares the output against a known-correct
answer. Deterministic, cheap, and blind to anything the reference didn't
anticipate.
:::

::: {.cat}
`<h4>Surface</h4>`{=html}
Token-overlap scores like BLEU/ROUGE. Cheap to compute, but grades word
choice, not correctness, the trap from the last section.
:::

::: {.cat}
`<h4>LLM-judge</h4>`{=html}
A model scores another model, against a rubric or in pairwise preference.
Handles open-ended output, at the cost of a rubric that must target truth.
:::
:::::

---

## Is the drop real?

::: {.def-box}
**Statistical significance (for a pass rate).** A pass rate is a
proportion; a proportion measured on a small sample has a wide confidence
interval. The same nominal drop can be noise at n=20 and signal at n=200,
report the interval, not just the rate.
:::

---


# Is the Drop Real? Statistical Significance

## Is the Drop Real? Statistical Significance {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/statistical-significance/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Is the Drop Real? Statistical Significance {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/statistical-significance/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Is the Drop Real? Statistical Significance {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/statistical-significance/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

# Part 2 · The pivot to observability {.center}

::: lead
A test set answers "did it get the known cases right." It cannot answer
"what happened on the live run nobody scripted." That takes watching, not
scoring: nested spans, token counts, cost, latency, read after the fact.
:::

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 260">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card ok" x="80" y="90" width="360" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="102" y="122">offline · scored</text>
    <text class="note" x="102" y="150" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">fixed inputs, known answers, one number</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card hot" x="560" y="90" width="360" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="582" y="122">online · traced</text>
    <text class="note" x="582" y="150" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px;fill:var(--amber)">live inputs, no known answer, a span tree</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 445 135 L 555 135"/>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="3">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="220">Same bot. Different question, different instrument.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

---

## Watching, not scoring

::: {.def-box}
**Trace / span.** A trace is the record of one real run; a span is one
step inside it (a model call, a tool call), with a start, a duration, and
its own attributes, nested under whichever span called it.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
Observability watches a live run with no answer key. The question changes
from "was this right" to "what happened, and what did it cost."
:::

---

## The two instruments, side by side

::::: {.taxonomy style="--cols:2"}
::: {.cat}
`<h4>Offline &middot; scored</h4>`{=html}
Fixed inputs, known-correct answers, a metric that is a pure function of
(prediction, expected). One number, comparable run to run.
:::

::: {.cat}
`<h4>Online &middot; traced</h4>`{=html}
Live inputs, no known answer, a nested span tree with tokens, cost, and
latency read after the fact. Not a score, a record of what happened.
:::
:::::

---


# The Pivot: Scored Offline, Traced Online

## The Pivot: Scored Offline, Traced Online {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/observability-the-pivot/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## The Pivot: Scored Offline, Traced Online {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/observability-the-pivot/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## The Pivot: Scored Offline, Traced Online {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/observability-the-pivot/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# A Real Trace: Spans, Tokens, Cost

## A Real Trace: Spans, Tokens, Cost {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/a-real-trace/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## A Real Trace: Spans, Tokens, Cost {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/a-real-trace/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## A Real Trace: Spans, Tokens, Cost {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/a-real-trace/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## A Real Trace: Spans, Tokens, Cost {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/a-real-trace/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# Drift Over Time: Same Eval, Two Model Versions

## Drift Over Time: Same Eval, Two Model Versions {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/drift-over-time/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Drift Over Time: Same Eval, Two Model Versions {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/drift-over-time/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Drift Over Time: Same Eval, Two Model Versions {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/drift-over-time/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## Drift Over Time: Same Eval, Two Model Versions {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/drift-over-time/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---

# Part 3 · Real tools, no stubs {.center}

::: lead
Three widely used libraries, run for real and deterministically: DeepEval's
own metric objects, Ragas's own non-LLM metrics, and the Langfuse SDK's own
span emission, captured offline with an in-memory OpenTelemetry exporter.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
A real library's own objects and formulas can run deterministically
without ever calling a hosted LLM, the point of Part 3 is the code path,
not the difficulty of the metric.
:::

::: {.src}
DeepEval docs · Ragas docs · Langfuse v4 (OpenTelemetry-based) docs
:::

---


# DeepEval, For Real

## DeepEval, For Real {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/deepeval-real-asserts/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## DeepEval, For Real {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/deepeval-real-asserts/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## DeepEval, For Real {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/deepeval-real-asserts/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## DeepEval, For Real {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/deepeval-real-asserts/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# Ragas: Real RAG Metrics

## Ragas: Real RAG Metrics {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/ragas-rag-metrics/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Ragas: Real RAG Metrics {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/ragas-rag-metrics/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Ragas: Real RAG Metrics {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/ragas-rag-metrics/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## Ragas: Real RAG Metrics {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/ragas-rag-metrics/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---


# Langfuse, Captured Offline

## Langfuse, Captured Offline {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/langfuse-trace/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Langfuse, Captured Offline {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/langfuse-trace/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Langfuse, Captured Offline {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/langfuse-trace/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## References

**Guides**
[Anthropic, Demystifying evals for AI agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents) · [OpenAI, A practical guide to building agents](https://openai.com/business/guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-ai-agents/) · [Anthropic, Building effective agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

**Libraries**
[DeepEval docs](https://deepeval.com/docs/getting-started) · [Ragas docs](https://docs.ragas.io/) · [Langfuse docs](https://langfuse.com/docs)

**Siblings**
[How Git Works, Visually](../../how-git-works/website/git.html) · [How RAG Works, Visually](../../how-rag-works/website/rag.html) · [How Inference & Serving Work, Visually](../../how-inference-and-serving-works/website/inference.html) · [How Agentic Harnesses Work, Visually](../../how-agentic-harnesses-work/website/harness.html) · [How LangChain & LangGraph Work, Visually](../../how-langchain-and-langgraph-work/website/langchain.html)

::: {.src}
Every figure in this deck: a real run of langchain 1.3 / langgraph 1.2 / deepeval / ragas / langfuse at Beanline Coffee, regenerable with build/regen_all.py · versions pinned in build/requirements.txt
:::
